title: Open Source AI Is Being Valued for Productivity, Flexibility, and Lower Lock-In
intent: Anchor anti-lock-in and side-by-side architecture arguments in recent organizational research rather than opinion.

source.title: Linux Foundation Europe Report Finds Open Source Drives Innovation and Digital Sovereignty, but Strategic Maturity Gaps Persist
source.methodology: Research based on insights from more than 300 European IT leaders.

fact.primary_stat: 63% of respondents said higher productivity was the top benefit of open source, followed by reduced vendor lock-in at 62% and lower cost of software ownership at 58%.
supporting_stats: 54% believed open source investment would most benefit industry standards and interoperability development.
41% reported adoption in AI and machine learning.
Only 34% maintained formal open source strategies, despite broad adoption.

business_relevance: The enterprise case for open ecosystems is not ideological; it is explicitly tied to productivity, cost, and flexibility.
Strategic immaturity remains a blocker even where technical adoption is already broad.
dzmitryi_commentary: This fits my positioning well. Side-by-side AI and automation should be designed so components can be replaced without rewriting the business process. In SAP support and operations, that means open interfaces, retrieval layers, event-driven contracts, and small replaceable services instead of platform-dependent custom monoliths.

provenance.note: Extracted and curated by Dzmitryi Kharlanau; enriched for attribution and crawler indexing.

meta.summary: Anchor anti-lock-in and side-by-side architecture arguments in recent organizational research rather than opinion.
